title: URL Shortener Design
problemStatement:
Design a URL shortening service similar to TinyURL or Bit.ly.

**Functional Requirements:**
- Given a long URL, generate a unique short URL
- When users access the short URL, redirect them to the original long URL
- Users can optionally set custom short links
- Links should expire after a configurable time period

**Non-Functional Requirements:**
- The system should be highly available
- URL redirection should happen in real-time with minimal latency
- Shortened links should not be predictable

**Scale:**
- 100M new URLs per month
- 10:1 read to write ratio (1B redirections per month)

requiredComponents: Base62/Base58 encoding or hash-based ID generation
keyTradeoffs: SQL vs NoSQL for storage — NoSQL is better for simple key-value lookups at scale
Hash-based vs counter-based short code generation
Cache eviction strategy — LRU for hot URLs
Handling hash collisions if using MD5/SHA truncation
antiPatterns: Using auto-increment IDs directly (predictable, security risk)
No caching layer (every redirect hits DB)

followUpTriggers.question: If a viral tweet contains one of your short URLs and it gets millions of clicks in an hour, how would your system handle that?
If I can guess your short URLs by incrementing the ID, what security implications does that have?
How would your system handle 100 million new URLs being created every month?

rubricFull.sampleAnswer: A URL shortener needs an API layer behind a load balancer, an encoding service that converts numeric IDs to Base62 strings, a NoSQL database (like DynamoDB or Cassandra) for O(1) key-value lookups, and a Redis cache for frequently accessed URLs. Use a distributed ID generator (Twitter Snowflake or Zookeeper-based counter ranges) to avoid collisions. The read path checks cache first, falls back to DB. For analytics, stream click events to Kafka for async processing.

scoringDimensions.name: API Design
scoringDimensions.criteria: Clean REST API with proper endpoints for create, redirect, and delete operations

scoringDimensions.name: Encoding Strategy
scoringDimensions.criteria: Sound approach for generating unique, non-predictable short codes (Base62, hashing)

scoringDimensions.name: Data Storage
scoringDimensions.criteria: Appropriate database choice with proper schema for high read throughput

scoringDimensions.name: Caching
scoringDimensions.criteria: Cache layer for hot URLs with proper eviction strategy

scoringDimensions.name: Scalability
scoringDimensions.criteria: Horizontal scaling plan, database sharding/partitioning strategy

scoringDimensions.name: Edge Cases
scoringDimensions.criteria: Handles collisions, expiration, custom aliases, and abuse prevention

hints: Think about the read-to-write ratio — this system is read-heavy. What does that imply for your architecture?
Consider using Base62 encoding (a-z, A-Z, 0-9) — how many characters do you need for 100M URLs?
What happens when two users submit the same long URL? Should they get the same short URL?
followUpQuestions: How would you implement analytics (click tracking, geographic data)?
How would you handle link expiration at scale?
How would you prevent abuse (spam URLs, phishing)?

notes: 7 Base62 characters give 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion unique codes — enough headroom for 100M/month for decades
Cache hit ratio target > 80% since 80% of traffic hits 20% of URLs (Pareto principle)
Snowflake IDs are 64-bit, time-sortable, and generated without coordination — ideal for distributed writes
DB schema: { shortCode (PK), longURL, userId, createdAt, expiresAt, customAlias? }
